purpose: Estimate the probability of disorderly repricing in opaque credit markets by applying historical base rates from analogous opaque sectors, given the absence of direct precedent for private credit at $1.7T scale.
components: Five historical analogues: S&Ls (1980s), SIVs (2007-08), junk bonds (1989-90), Japanese jusen (1990s), AT1 CoCos (2023)
Base rate outcome: 60-80% experienced disorderly repricing
Median lag from opacity to repricing: 4-5 years
Three spillover channels: semi-liquid vehicle redemptions (~$300B BDCs/interval funds), insurance RBC rebalancing (~$700B exposure), information contagion from first forced mark-to-market
Observable leading indicator: PIK toggle usage (currently 5-8%, up from ~2% in 2022)
current_assessment: Private credit at $1.7T is outside all public indices and financial conditions measures. PIK toggle usage trending up. Mark-to-recovery gap unknown (5-25% range). Base rate suggests eventual disorderly repricing is more likely than not, but median lag of 4-5 years means timing is highly uncertain. This is the highest-importance, lowest-precision risk in credit-equity linkage.
limitations: All five analogues are structurally dissimilar to current private credit
60-80% repricing rate from 5 cases is not statistically robust
Median lag of 4-5 years is operationally unhelpful
Mark-to-recovery gap range of 5-25% is too wide to be actionable
The analogues predate current regulatory frameworks and market structures
